informal_statement.text: Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.
preconditions: the input tree is a valid BST
both query keys are present in the tree
required_properties: the returned key appears in the tree
both query keys are in the subtree rooted at the returned key
no proper descendant of the returned key has both query keys in its subtree
edge_cases: both keys equal (returned key equals them)
one key is an ancestor of the other
keys on opposite sides of the root
tree with only a single path

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST.
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree.
common_failure_modes: returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key.
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys.
common_failure_modes: using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  (∃ t : Tree, t = tree) → True
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: No explicit precondition is asserted in the code, but the module comment states: both keys are present in the tree. We will capture this as a separate correctness obligation below.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
let lo : Int := if p ≤ q then p else q
let hi : Int := if p ≤ q then q else p
in lo ≤ hi ∧ (lo = min p q) ∧ (hi = max p q)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The implementation normalizes the inputs so that lo = min(p,q) and hi = max(p,q), ensuring lo ≤ hi.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree),
  BST tree →
  ∀ (x : Int),
    x ∈ keys tree →
    (match tree with
     | Nil => False
     | Node l k r =>
         (x = k ∨ (x < k ∧ x ∈ keys l) ∨ (x > k ∧ x ∈ keys r)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: To justify the branch decisions (go left if hi < k, go right if lo > k), we need the tree to satisfy the BST ordering: all keys in the left subtree are < k and all keys in the right subtree are > k.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  let lo := min p q
  let hi := max p q
  in
  ∀ (cur : Tree),
    cur ∈ subtree tree →
    (p ∈ keys cur ∧ q ∈ keys cur) →
    (match cur with
     | Nil => False
     | Node left k right =>
         (if hi < k then (p ∈ keys left ∧ q ∈ keys left)
          else if lo > k then (p ∈ keys right ∧ q ∈ keys right)
          else (lo ≤ k ∧ k ≤ hi)))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: At each step, assuming both keys are still in the current subtree, the BST property implies: if hi < k then both keys must be in the left subtree; if lo > k then both keys must be in the right subtree; otherwise k lies between lo and hi.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  let lo := min p q
  let hi := max p q
  in
  ∀ (cur : Tree),
    cur ∈ subtree tree →
    (∃ n : Nat,
      runSteps tree p q n = Some (lca_bst tree p q))
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The loop terminates by descending the tree: each iteration moves to a strict subtree (left or right) and eventually reaches Nil or returns Some(k).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int) (r : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some r →
  r ∈ keys tree ∧
  (r isAncestorOf tree p) ∧
  (r isAncestorOf tree q)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If the function returns Some r, then r is a key in the tree and is an ancestor of both p and q.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int),
  BST tree →
  p ∈ keys tree →
  q ∈ keys tree →
  ∃ r : Int, lca_bst tree p q = some r
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Under the documented assumption that both keys are present in the BST, the search will not fall off the tree and will return Some.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q r : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some r →
  (r isAncestorOf tree p ∧ r isAncestorOf tree q) ∧
  ∀ (s : Int),
    s ≠ r →
    (s isAncestorOf tree p ∧ s isAncestorOf tree q) →
    (depth tree s < depth tree r)
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The returned key r is the lowest (deepest) node that is a common ancestor of p and q.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (tree : Tree) (p q : Int) (k : Int),
  BST tree →
  lca_bst tree p q = some k →
  min p q ≤ k ∧ k ≤ max p q
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Because the algorithm returns when lo ≤ k ≤ hi at some node, the returned key must lie between the two query keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
∀ (p q : Int),
  lca_bst Nil p q = none
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If the tree is empty, the function returns None.
